speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I have a little bit of background on how you came to, well before you came to Gold Mill. The first thing I noticed on that background sheet you have, you grew up in South Dakota and in Saskatchewan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your parents homesteading?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They were farmers in South Dakota and then they homesteaded in Saskatchewan. I was a farmer's boy until I came to Spokane. That was the end of the farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your whole family leave Saskatchewan?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I left just a few months before them and went back to South Dakota for, well I opened about July to December. I worked there and then I joined them in Spokane. They came to Spokane I'd say about in October of that year. Yeah, they sold out.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Then I, I said there, I went to that telegraph school.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Spokane Telegraph Institute it was called. Finished that and then started working for the railroads. Great Northern. I worked right in Spokane for a while right down there on Havermale Island where the fair now is. The building where that tower is left. Oh, I worked quite a few places on the Great Northern. Priest River, Newport. West, I worked at Harrington.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Never got as far as Wenatchee. Edwall, Lyons. Well then, the next spring things were kind of, there wasn't that much work for an extra board and a new man. You see you're on the extra board. So this Inland Empire which run between Spokane, Moscow, and Colfax, they needed somebody so I went to work for them. And I said in there I was cashier and operator different places and relief agent. And wound up in Moscow, cashier and operator where I stayed for about two years. And then this job at Bovill came up, joint agent. It was a better paying job. So I just quipped and took it. Railroad to Moscow, they were very good about it too. When I was leaving they said, well we moved you in here. Do that you know, your furniture, anything you have. We'll move you out. So I had free transportation of my household goods, myself, family. That's when I started into Bovill.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well things were humming there then. The roads into Bovill in the wintertime there just wasn't any. There was snow covered and they never tried to keep them open. People would put their cars up on blocks. Sometimes as early as October, November, whenever winter happened set in. And they were there until the next May or more. I heard some say that was a pretty good thing. They got a chance to catch up on their grocery bill. That wasn't spending so much for gasoline. But the depot, every time the trains come in, which was shortly after noon or something. Milwaukee came up there passing your train. The WI&M passing your train met. And mail and everything that came into the town came on the train. So quite often there was a lot of people gathered around the depot. It just seemed to be a daily event. They gathered around there to see the trains come in and go. Two to three train teams backed up against the platform to take the mail, express, whatnot. And they delivered over town.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: When the winter is there, sometimes the snow used to get real deep. We still have snow there, but it's nothing like it was in those days. It seems to me that about, oh, by the 15th of December, that fence that was out in front of the depot, it was a good five and a half feet high. But that would be covered. The posts would disappear. And you wouldn't see them again until March, maybe, when the snow started to go down. And the railroads, it was quite a job there plowing out the tracks, the side tracks. They had all kinds of equipment to do it. Sometimes three or four engines stalled, snowed in. Take one to pull another one out, they get going.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But, oh, they get through it. And it was the same every winter there for quite a while. And then, finally, the winter wasn't quite so severe. Less snow.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Of course, sometimes the logging company would be down for a few months in the winter. In those days, but now it's kind of reversed. They log down in the winter, but the spring comes and they have their layoff for maybe a couple of months. Things are wet in the woods. Those days it was all horse logging. And that was something they had, Potlatch Lumber Company, which was called at the time, they had, oh, two or three huge barns across the meadow in the depot, where they would keep their horses when they were not working. And they never had a horse that wouldn't weigh at least a ton. And a lot of them weigh in 21 and 2200 pounds. Big horses. But I guess it takes big horses to do heavy work. Woods.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then all at once there was no more horses that started using cats. They sold those horses if they could. Farmers from around the country would come in and buy them. I think they said they had 260 head there at one time in the winter. That's a lot of horses. Took a fair -sized crew of men to just take care of them. But then they knew anything to get rid of them. They'd sell them for whatever people wanted to give. And then some even they shipped, loaded them in stock cars and shipped to Portland, I think it was. Where they would be slaughtered and I don't know what they would be used for. They used to say that they were going to the glue factory.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Or what.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be in the lumberjacks in the winter or over?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: A lot of them would come to Spokane. This was kind of the headquarters scene. A lot of the single lumberjacks would come to Spokane. Of course the family people and some of the others would remain there in Volvo in a shack or two. The edges of town would be shacking up different ones. Just waiting for spring. But the big majority of those single men they'd hang out in Spokane. But they always kept in touch and next to spring when it opened they'd be back.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So you really had a lot of continuity from one year to the other. A lot of the same people. Oh yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah. Yeah they did well. The foreman, the superintendent and all of them, they get to know certain lumberjacks. They know they do the work. They like them. They like to have them. Oh lumberjack, it's a lumberjack and they just don't make them anymore. No, there's no lumberjacks because they were the single men.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And for quite a number of years now, the woodwork is done by young married people. They drive their cars to work. They drive as close as they can to the work. But oh yeah, those old, a lot of them were sawyers. That's where they used the crosscut saws. One on each end of it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That's a thing of the past too. Trees are all felled now by power saws.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So that element which isn't there anymore. One young fellow with a power saw knocked down more trees in half a day than the, well I don't know how many good men with a hand saw could do in two or four days. Just that much difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was being tough, being able to get out there, take it and put in a real long day's work, was that all part of, was that really important to the lumberjacks themselves?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, I don't think so, you know. No, the weather was never that bad. It worked long hours. But when you go back, well you're going back about 49 years now, seeing that I came to Bovill in 1925, everybody worked longer hours.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Even when I left the depot at Moscow, I was working ten hour days. When I went to Bovill, however, it was eight hours. And the eight hour day then in the woods was coming in, but oh they worked long hours. A lot of them, you see, sawyers, and more than sawyers, it was gyppo. So much a foot, so much a thousand. So they didn't mind the long hours. But the more they worked, the more they made. There would always be a considerable percentage of the group that was on hourly pay. So it was a pretty small pay to then about, oh, 32 and a half cents an hour was probably a pretty good pay then. And a lot of the extra gangs on the railroad was only 25 cents an hour.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So, no, it didn't take any super person physically to, of course, doing that kind of work, you know. So you get used to it, and put out a lot of work, eat heavy. All the little lumberjacks, for the most of them, they could stay in the woods just about so long, and then they had to get out of there. They'd get, what did they call that, some kind of fever. Not that they were sick, but they had to get out and go to town in some place where they called timber. Of course, in those days when I went there, it was a prohibition, so there wasn't any place around there that they could do that. I guess maybe that's what attracted some of them to Spokane in the winter. Probably easier to... Oh yeah, it was a pretty lumberjack to have a pretty good stake and go to town and go broke. And if he was known, he could borrow quite a little bit. And then after he spent that, well, then he'd be ready to go back to work. And do the same thing over, and over and over. They were regarded as pretty honorable men. There didn't seem to be any little bad ones at all. They were good. Help everybody they could. But, well, they never worked their day in the woods. You heard lots of stories about them, you know, that they would... Some of them, they would dine their socks, and they'd roll a sock when they started to wear it out on the heel. They'd roll it a little ways, or roll it again, roll it, keep rolling it, until finally it would cut right in two. Well, then they'd have to get it in a pair of socks. They would do that, but still when they'd get to town, money was not. Free timber. What a treat, everybody. And there was really no fight started amongst the lumberjacks. They didn't do that. Those extra gangs they used to have on the railroad, though, that was a little different. But every year there'd be an extra gang come up, and they'd have their own cars on the railroad, bunk cars. Probably about a hundred men in the camp. And they would do, renew ties. That was the main work. But they picked those up from everywhere, you know, get them fresh out of jail or whatnot. Just go out there, it was 25 cents an hour. But they did get their board. They'd take the place to sleep. So they were coming and going all the time. Some of those were pretty rough characters. I guess they didn't... One of them I looked out the window there to the depot one time. I see a laying head across the rail up near the water tank. And there was a train coming pretty soon. So I hiked up there. It wasn't too far. He was dead drunk. I pulled him off the rail. I pulled him by the feet. He woke up a little bit then and I told him, I said, Well, you got your neck right over that rail. I said, there's a train coming along here pretty quick. I said, that'll just cut your head off. Oh, he says, that'd be all right. And I think he meant it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, I dragged him around the back of the water tank. And it seemed like he kind of went back to sleep. And I went to the depot and kept my eye peeled. It was only five minutes or so until the train came. And they couldn't have stopped.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, that was a different class. There were different class of people all together. But they were pretty armable men. The lumberjack.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But from that old crap, retired, died off, adjusted any more of them.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And old Pat Malone, who probably knew every one of them.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Superintendent there, Noggle, C .G. Noggle, he knew a lot of them personally.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And liked them. He knew they'd break loose once in a while and go on and on and on. But anyway, when you get them back in the woods, they were good men.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Along with them. There's one old timer there. Ted Collins. He was a lumberjack. But, oh, he decided to leave Bovill.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They'd had him in jail there for a while. More or less to sober him up. And, well again, I was just the piece. And sometime I used to hold court right in the depot. And that time I did. It was Ted, just being drunk. And he was looking out and doing a train going by with log flats. He said, if I was free, I'd go right out there and jump on one of those log flats. And get out of here.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I said, is that what you want to do, Ted?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yes. I said, go right ahead.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He said, you mean I'm free to do that?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Go right ahead, that's what you want to do. And he did. He didn't do nothing. He didn't have a dime. No need to find him. No need to put him back in jail. All he did was being drunk. He didn't harm anybody else. So, and he was gone for quite a while. I don't know where he went to, but... Oh, it must have been a year afterwards. In Moscow there was a man, they found a man dead in a boxcar. And we had a few people that had lived in Bovil that then was in Moscow. And one of them identified that man as Ted Collins. I said, that's Ted. Well, then when that was the case, they asked some of us to... at Bovil if we would come down and see what we thought. Well, I'm surprised, there was quite a few that knew him much better and longer than I did. They said, yeah, that's Ted Collins. But I myself couldn't see it because I... Well, first of all, when I went over there, Howard Short was... But his wife was there at night. She said, we got the body down in the basement and we can go down and see him. And we looked at him and we looked from one angle and... Just a dead ringer for him. You go around the other side and it wasn't. And I asked her if she had a tape measure there. Yeah, so we measured him. And he wasn't as... He wasn't as near as tall as I had figured these Ted Collins to be. So they had you say a little something there, you know, if you thought it was Ted Collins or if you knew who it was or... Well, I wrote down, I don't know who it is, but I'm satisfied it's not Ted Collins. Well, another year or so went by and by gosh, here old Ted rolls off of a flat car, walked in the depot. I wasn't too surprised because I was satisfied in my own mind that that wasn't Ted Collins that was buried in Moscow. And I told him, well, I said, Ted, you've been dead now for, oh, let's see, oh, one or two years.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh? He said, where did they bury me? Moscow?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, well, well. He says, I ought to be good for a meal then. I said, you bet you will be. And I said, you just go over there to Mrs. Watts. She was running her essence. And I said, you'll get a meal. And she was so glad to see him that she just kept him there working around, bull cooking. He stayed there for a long, long time.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Sounds like dying was one of the best things that ever happened to him. Yeah.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah. But I think I, you know, a little more detail about examining him and his clothes. He had different clothes, you know, than a lumberjack would have. You know, I liked shoes and little black cotton socks. Lumberjacks, you know, and he got to generally wool socks and wool pants and whatnot. So his clothing didn't check out to me either.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then when I measured him for height, and I asked about that too, now that this man is dead, how would he be if he was living?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They said, taller. Laying on that slab. In a way, we were measuring him. He'd be taller than if he was walking around. And you can see that too, you know. So that was the last convincing point to me. Yeah. Oh yeah, well, it was one of those old fellows that liked Ted. Oh yeah. Yeah. Ted was probably one of the most likable ones though. He was happy -go -lucky. He didn't bother anybody. But he never wanted to sober up. He'd get drunk, he wanted to stay that way. But then they always said when he got in the woods, he was as good a man as they had. And the drink didn't bother him at all until he'd come to town again. I think he was a teamster. I'm quite sure he was. I should have had lots of teamsters, you know.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yeah. Lots of other characters, you know, that you...

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: You haven't thought of them for a while, it kind of skips your mind.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The woods was full of them. Of course, that depression came along then. You know, it shut down and... Oh, I don't know exactly, but I suppose... 31. They never didn't turn a wheel for... probably a year and a half or more.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It was probably about 31 when they pulled the bin. A whole lot of people moved away then.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Things were pretty hard. There was no unemployment compensation, no social security. That's all ahead of that. They didn't have a dime. People, generally speaking, didn't save a head. They would always, most of them, be good at the grocery store for 60, 90 days or more credit. But then that was running out and this was different, you know. When the company pulled the pin, it was early in the fall. It wasn't for winter coming on, so it was a different deal. That was pretty tough going there. However, people seemed to enjoy living there. Nobody had a dime.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I was fortunate in having a job that I did have before that. I had a cashier and a warehouseman. But the business was down so bad that they laid both of those off. And I was running the job alone. But, as I say, I was very fortunate to have been on the payroll all the time. And there were very few school teachers.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: A few people taking care of the horses. And he about had it. Oh yeah, there was a few on the railroad section for them. A couple of men.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They were looking pretty black there. I know how it was because, I guess it was because I had a job that they made me relief director. I only had a limited amount of money, very little to keep the town going. And you just about have to go into everybody's house to talk to them and see what you could do to see, you know, Maybe they had some canned fruit or something that another family didn't have. Well, you wouldn't ask them to share, but then you knew better how to distribute the money trying to keep everybody alive. So, it was about the most rewarding work I believe I ever had. Self -satisfaction. I don't know. I don't really think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it work out? How did you manage to distribute things?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, you just had to form the committee of cars and list everybody in town. And then we'd have so much money. And there was a little work, you see, WPA.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But they were, I don't know, slow in getting that going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when that got going, then, see, I don't know, like, what would it be allocated so much money a month for this work?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then you'd have to say people with the larger family, like some of them, have many six kids. Well, a man like that, he'd have to work more days. Maybe he'd work four days a week out of the six. And then somebody that maybe just a man wide. Well, two days at the most. There wasn't too many single men. They got out of there. The ones that didn't, they kind of had to work for themselves. But they mostly got out of there where they went to up here, I guess. But that's the way you had to divide it. Try to keep everybody alive. And we could set it up for anything that had to be approved. But what we done was road work. Grading and whatnot. Our work there was mostly between Volvo and Elk River. First didn't go clear to Elk River. Out there about seven miles.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, it was kind of bad. We didn't have anything to work with. But, uh, Font National Amber Company was very good on loaning us their equipment without charge. And they would have scrapers, you know, and picks and that. Cross cut saws and the TV's and shovels. It was more or less hand work. Anybody that had a team, we had them working, call them Rock. With a little wagon of some kind and some on the scrapers. But, uh, well, we got something done anyway. But the jobs were manufactured, you know, just because... ...we had to get some money to people and that was one way of them working for it. In our case, the road work we done worked out pretty good because... ...well, we had some real good men. Some of them had worked in the woods. They could do about most anything. And build a bridge just out of, you know, fall it, timber, trim it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They knew just how to go at it. And, uh, we did get enough rock on that road. It was a problem with the highway district. And I was the secretary treasurer of that highway district, number four. And I know how short they were on money. They owed bonds. Didn't hardly pay the interest, though they didn't have any money. And we had to maintain that road. But through this work, we got the road widened enough and a little sprinkling of rock on it... ...to a point to where the state then accepted the road as part of the state system. Well, that relieved us, the highway district, from maintaining it. Which was a big thing for us, the highway district.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Quite helpful, money -wise. And then the state, kind of piecemeal, they would extend it a couple of miles or so. I think now they've run over it just lately, but I believe it's a fair road all the way through.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It's the Bovill Elk River Road. Yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It is. It's pretty good now.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Is it all hard top?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No, not yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it would seem to me that it would be harder, maybe, in a place like Bovill... ...than in a more farming area where you've got all your produce pretty much right to hand on the farms... ...and everybody's growing his own garden.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So it would be harder for food purposes in Bovill. Oh yes, Bovill, see that's turned up. No, it's gone. No, Bovill was a poor place to raise any food, you know. It's not all that high, it is high, but... ...only about ten miles down there to Derry and they could raise about anything they wanted to. But Bovill, it's the nights that were so cold that... ...oh, you couldn't think of growing potatoes, never. Or tomatoes. All the late years, some of them seemed to raise strawberries, but for a long time there... ...nobody ever tried to raise strawberries. So there wasn't much gardening going on, a little bit, but very little. And you might say no farming at all.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: A couple of places on the edges of town, maybe raise little oats or hay or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how the merchants did in Bovill during the Depression?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, they didn't do good at all, eventually had to close up. Generally speaking. No, they'd get behind on the taxes, you know, they couldn't pay the taxes. And most of them went out of business. There was one store there, though, John Grove. They were in real bad shape, real bad financially. And John Grove passed away, even his wife took over. Hung on for a while and... ...she was threatened there too with the county tax collector. They'd have to do something, they'd have to take the property or something for the taxes. But they held that off for a while and then got out far enough to where business started to pick up a little bit. And that war scare, of course, in Europe. So that must have been in 1939, I guess. Things started picking up pretty well from there on and then, of course, got into World War II and... ...sell anything you had. So they got one place, come out of it in good shape. But the others more or less went out of business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the families hang on until Potlatch started up again?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, well, during that period a lot of them worked in the CCCs.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That was... I should know what CCCs were. Civilian Conservation Corps. That's right, that's right. So a lot of them, especially anybody that worked in a supervisory capacity... ...such as foreman or assistant foreman or anything like that... ...it was easy for them to get on in the CCCs as foremen. So a lot of them got pretty fair jobs, well, you know, fair as of the time. And then the others also got on as laborers. So the town pretty well was taken up by the CCCs. And over the spell, how long it lasted, a year and a half or whatever it was...

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: ...before the lumber company went back to work. So there were some people that, of course, moved away. There were quite a few of them. It was the way it never came back. Generally speaking, there was a lot of them that stayed there and stuck it out. And it came out all right.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yes, it was a bad period.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I hope it never happens again. Sometimes you never know.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, to look at some bright, more pleasant things...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...could you tell me what you remember about Pat Malone?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, old Pat, everybody in town loved him, you know, generally speaking. I don't know how long he'd been on the sheriff's force... ...because he was there when I came. But I think it was quite a number of years before that.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But he was deputy sheriff. And he had all the authority, the resting authority, that any deputy sheriff would have. However, he was situated there in Boville. He would report things, of course, that he figured should be reported to the sheriff's office in Moscow. That's mostly, I suppose, Pat's work, because he didn't make too many arrests. If he did, generally speaking, somebody got a little too drunk. But he lived there in a Spokane hotel. Dave Ellison and his wife, Sophie, from the hotel had to live right there, both sleeping and eating. In the county paid him just a token amount. One time, I know, it was $30 a month. But he did have his board in Rome. So, old Pat, with that $30, as long as it would last, he'd be treating the kids going by... ...ice cream cone or pop, for example. Until the 30s he'd be gone, and then he'd be broke for the rest of the month. But he didn't need any money, because he had a place to eat and sleep. But he'd sit there in the window with the corn cob pipe in his mouth. Well, that was something. There was a law enforcement agent. It helped, I know it helped. The county was lucky to have him there.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But, oh, Pat could tell some tall tales about things that happened in his life.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: All amusing. And well could have happened. He was a big man. And I'll bet you when he was young, there wasn't too many that wanted to tangle with him either. First when I first knew him, he was getting old.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But, oh yeah, he was... He was death on woodleggers, moonshiners. That seemed to be his...

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: ...main line of work there during the professional period.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yeah, they used to pull tricks on him too. But generally speaking, Pat was well liked by everybody. Nobody took him too serious.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But it was all right. It was well worth a little cost in keeping there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see, when you sit playing tricks on Pat, is there any kind of practical jokes?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yeah, but I don't know. Those better stay like they are.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No,

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: to my knowledge, Pat didn't drink. I daresay that he did when he was younger, but he... ...in my time knowing him, I never knew him to drink.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But I suppose he did in his younger years.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, every once in a while, you know, he'd get a call. He'd go, Boss, go look out for somebody.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And he'd get... it'll work like that. I know there was something or other in the area...

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: ...just what happened there, but they were watching for somebody. Pat was down on the highway at Helmer this night. The family and I was coming home and stopped me. But he was stopping all cars. And of course, as soon as he'd look in the car and know who you were, oh, okay. Well, that was a job for him. I forget just what that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the stories, any of the tall tales that Pat used to tell?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, the one that he used to tell me, it seemed about every year or so... ...he'd come over and tell me the same story. Of course, I know he'd forget that he'd said it. I don't know the start of this either, but they were two desperados that... ...and he got the word that they were coming from Clarkie on a passenger train. He was over there. But they jumped off on the opposite side of the train and started to cross the meadow. And by the time he got around to the end of the car, they were running, and he said he took a shot at one. And he said, I'm pretty sure I hit him. But I didn't get him, though, because by the time I got over there, he was gone and the other fellow was gone. But I know I hit him. I know I hit him. But it was a long story. Old Pat, he'd tell me the details of what he's done, and it stuck in my mind because... ...well, while I'd be listening, I'd be doing my own work at the same time. And I think, you know, it's about time for old Pat to come over and tell me that same story again. And sure enough... ...but I never let on to him that I'd ever heard it before. It was all old news to me. And then when he gets through, get up, go out, go... ...back on the job. Well, his job was sitting there in that hotel. Had a big window there. He sat right in there in one of the leather rocking chairs.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, that's the one I remember telling about. Oh, of course, that time... Well, this was the truth about the time the safe was stolen out of the depot.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And old Pete Caldwell, the conductor, woke him up, told him... ...he got up, the first thing he'd do is put that corn cob in his mouth, of course... ...and get his shoes tied. And this was in June. And Pete Caldwell stayed there while Pat was getting dressed. Old Pat says it'd be gibberish if there was just a fresh snow. But there wasn't. But then he called me and he called Sam Peterson, the banker. I don't know why he called him, but he did. By the time I got down to the depot, Sam was there too, and Pat...

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: ...you could see what happened. These people broke that window out. And they broke it out completely. Took it out. So it just looked like a clean window instead of a broken window. Inside the doors all opened from the inside the safe. It weighed, I think, 1350 pounds, I believe, is what the safe weighed. And it had casters on it, and they rolled it out on the platform. And they got a pushcar from the tool house. And with it, the gangplank just rolled onto the pushcar... ...and pushed it down the WI&M, which was a little bit down grade. Well, that old Pat guessed that that's where they'd have to go on with that... ...because there's no motor or anything on the pushcar. They had to push it by hand, but slightly down grade. They could do that. So then, well, better get the section boss. His name was Charlie Eller. Charlie Eller had to get the gas car out and go down there. Well, then Charlie, he had to call his whole crew. He had about four men, laborers. He had to get them out. This was probably three o 'clock in the morning. And so he got to going, and old Pat stepped right on the front with the flashlight... ...and the gun drawn, going down through those cuts. And here was the safe. Oh, two and a half miles, I suppose, down. They had rolled it off to the side and blew it. There wasn't much money in it. But, and there was express money orders, but in blowing it... ...the explosion burnt the money orders, so they didn't try to take those at all. Otherwise, I think they would have and tried to pass them. But a little burnt marked right along the edge of all of them, so they left those. They just took what cash was there. They didn't take any checks. The tickets, card tickets, was blowing up in the trees for 25 feet or more. We recovered most of the tickets. That was no loss anyway. We had enough, but by shaking the trees for a couple days afterwards, you get more tickets down. I believe there were very, very few tickets lost, but no, they didn't see anything of any men. See, Pat had his gun drawn all the way down. Oh yes, he stepped right on the front of the gas car with his gun drawn all the way down. Oh, it would have been so easy if they had been bad men and wanted to kill all of us. They could have done it, we were just sitting ducks going down through those cuts and trees on each side. One side anyway was all trees. Oh, I never thought at the time how dangerous it was. But afterwards I sized it up, my lord. But, oh, it was for two or three nights there. Gangs of us organized to watch out around the edge of town. That those fellas would be coming back in because they would have to get somewhere for food. But nobody showed up. But, yeah, Pat thought, I think this was over near Park. It was a schoolhouse over there. I think Pat was with some of the sheriff's force that kind of had a lead over there. And there was somebody, I guess, running across the field. And again old Pat said he took a shot. He didn't get him, he was too far. But I think I hit him. I think I hit him.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, that was the one experience I had with Pat. Because all they always had, he had a lot of grit. But there was a lot of foolishness there too. I could see afterwards the way we done that job.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I wonder if Pat was drinking and keeping it from you. I mean, I wonder, because I hear a number of stories that make it sound like if he wasn't drinking he sure was letting his friends get away with it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, oh yeah, there was stories, you know, too little bit about Pat doing his share of drinking. But as I say, I never seen it. I never seen any effects on him. But I didn't see him all the time either. Sure, I see him every day.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: There's one story I can still remember. Somebody told me that one of these Chautauquas came through and they, you know, it was a medicine show I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had one of the jokes he said is, what's an island?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The answer is, the city marshal surrounded by kegaboos.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah,

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I think Pat for a while there, that's kind of before my time too, he was I guess kind of city marshal as well. I think the village may have paid him something. But that wasn't the case when I was there. In Strictly County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about that last time you saw him?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yes, that time I didn't know that he was even sick. But it was long about 11 o 'clock in the forenoon. Mrs.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Ellison called me at the depot, would I stop and see Pat on the way home for lunch?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And I said yes, and I did. I went into that little room where he stayed and he was in bed and half crying. And all that was on his mind was, he didn't want to be buried in Poppersfield. That's just the way he'd say it. I don't want to be buried in Poppersfield. Well, I assured him that that would never happen.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And he questioned me a little bit about, you're sure about it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No, yes, I said that. It'll never happen, Pat. And well, then he felt better and he threw the covers back and he sat on the edge of the bed. He seemed to me he felt a good deal better. He was relieved of something he'd been worrying about. And well, when I got home, which was only a few minutes, I called the sheriff's office in Moscow and told them about talking with old Pat and he was sick and in bed. So they said, well, we will send a car up and get him. And they did. And when they had him in the car and started out of town, he told them, he said, that's my last trip up that hill. There's a grade going out of all the report, dearie. My last trip up that hill.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, the next day I called the sheriff's office to see how Pat was. And they said, no, the doctors can't find a thing wrong with him. All he needs is a little hospitalization and we'll keep him here for a day or two. He'll be all right. Well, that would have been on Saturday. It was Friday that he took him over. This was Saturday. And then Sunday, the family and I went to Lewiston to a Lewiston roundup. There was another couple with us. I told them, we want to go back home through Moscow and see old Pat. Okay. So we went back through Moscow, arrived there, I suppose, eight o 'clock or such. And old Pat was dead. He had died. So I guess he knew more about the doctors. He knew that was his last trip up that hill. And they didn't know it. So he had a nice funeral in Moscow where he was buried. And then a little later I circulated a petition around amongst his friends in Volvo for a headstone.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And got a price on one from Sturtivant in Moscow. And figured out the people in Volvo that would give toward it. And come down and figured out about if I could get five dollars apiece from the ones that I thought I could, that would be enough. And one exception. Everybody I contacted, yes, lied to. Five dollars. And one fellow says, well now if you don't get enough, come back and I'll give you more. That was Dave Ellison. But we got enough on the first run. So he has a headstone today. My kids still, they thought a lot of old Pat. And every Memorial Day when we go to the cemetery after we've taken care of the two graves we have there now, the family, the next first place they head for is old Pat's. That's the flowers on old Pat's grave. He's well remembered by a lot of people.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, I think that was honest enough. And he done his job, however, it was in his way. That's the way it would have to be. It had to be his way. I think he had been a policeman earlier in life. Well, for a short time in New York City.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But then, again in, where? Was it Duluth?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Duluth.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Duluth, absolutely. That's it. Duluth. Duluth, Minnesota. Yeah, he worked there quite a while I guess. Well, at that time I think it was probably logging around Duluth. Quite sure. It was lumbering logging. And apparently he attached to that. Maybe that's why he drifted west. I don't know. I don't remember now what got him out west.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But yeah, that's why I'm glad you said that because well known.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I've heard it from a couple of people. That's where I got it. I've heard other places too, but Duluth is the one. Yeah.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I guess he was probably there for quite a while because he talked a lot about Duluth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the time you mentioned to me when you as Justice of the Peace there weren't tough enough for Pat's liking?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yeah, there was one time there.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: This Nick the Greek, I call him. He was bootlegging. He wasn't moonshining, but he was bootlegging. Pat was trying to catch him. And he'd had a couple of wraps before that for bootlegging. And this time Pat searched his little cabin where he was staying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was his second shack, what do you want to call it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: From floor to top. He came up with two half pints of moonshine. That's all he could find in Nick's place. But then he took them in for that for possession. That was the law to possession of transportation or manufacture any of that was unlawful. This was for possession. There was only two half pints. Well, I could see that the prohibition was just about to be revealed. It was. It was too long afterwards. I didn't feel too strongly about it then. But he asked old Pat, who wanted me to give that Greek a jail sentence. And that would have been his third time and it would have been pretty severe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Pat wanted you to give him a jail sentence?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, Pat wanted me to give him a jail sentence and that would be the third time for him and he'd get quite a jolt. But I couldn't see it that way. Two half pints. I think I give Nicky's choice a fine or jail sentence. I think I said $50 a half a pint. And that would have made his $100 because he had two half pints. Or 50 days in jail for each half pint. That would have been 100 days. Well, I knew, I was pretty sure, that this Dick had the money. And I wasn't wrong on that. But, well, he elected to pay the fine. And old Pat was shaking his head all the time about that. He didn't like that. And Nick says, well, I haven't got the money on me. No, I said, I didn't expect you to. You can get it, Shirley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he says, can I go and get it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I said, sure. Go and get the money. And why it was no time. An hour he was back with the money. But old Pat, he cussed a little bit under his breath as he was going out the door. No, he didn't like that. He wanted that guy to go to jail. But this fellow wasn't a bad guy at all. Sure, he was selling a little moonshine, but he wasn't selling to kids or anybody like that.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then I knew that it was in my own mind that it was going to be legal this in a short time. But no, Pat was disgusted with me there for a while. He didn't visit with me very much for a little while after that. But oh, and long he got over it. And he never talked about it again. He was just as friendly to me afterwards as he was before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Pat go after many moonshiners, do you think? Or were the guys pretty much left alone unless they sort of got in his way?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No, it was the other way around. It seemed to me that old Pat liked to go after them. That was one thing he seemed that he must have liked to do.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He'd have to have a fresh nose. Well, he thought he was a pretty good detective himself, but really he'd have to have a fresh nose. I think that's where Pat did pretty good.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But yeah, he was pretty good at scaring some of them too. Whether he scared them or not would be another thing, but he'd always think he'd scare them out.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, he... well, I don't know. I suppose he got a moonshiner or two in his life. But mostly his was for bootlegging. It would be bottle men, you'd see around selling. To catch a moonshiner, you'd have to get out in the woods someplace, you know, where they had a cabin or something like that. You'd see a lot of smoke, heavy smoke coming out of the chimney for a long period of time. You'd know that somebody in there was brewing something, cooking that mash.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah. It always seemed to me, and others too there, that that was one of Pat's delights. He could really catch a moonshiner or a bootlegger. That was something he liked to do.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I suppose that everybody knew him so well, they'd just stay out of his way, and that was their business. Oh, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Some of them said they could follow right behind them to see where Pat was going, then they could walk behind. They'd be free. But he never looked back. I don't know. Oh, you'd hear things like that, you know. They'd find out they liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you see much of it in town?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: You say that when the lumberjacks came into town, it was often to get drunk. This guy coming out of camp with some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see much of it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No. Because of all the pool halls, there was a couple of them, soft drinks, pool tables, card games. But, oh, they wouldn't have allowed it at all, you know. Because there was quite a penalty, you know, and you could shut them up, put them out of bed, and it's, oh, no. It was never allowed in those places, and you didn't see it in there. No, those guys, and I think really the talk about the lumberjack and going to town and getting drunk was before prohibition. Sure, you see at one time there was lots of saloons and bowls. I never seen them because of it. Oh, I never seen them there. But there were, and in those days, that's what would happen. And every other little town would be the same way. Ploose was quite a place. They said, I worked in Ploose for a while for the railroad. I was in a long main street right from the North Pacific people up and back, and I don't know how many saloons, something like, oh, gosh, 20 or more. 27, I believe they said at one time, if you made the rounds. The depot got to the end of the street and back down. But, if Ploose was a kind of a jumping off place too at one time, the railroad, and that's where you get the railroad to go up the WI&M to the logging countries, you see. It's very mobile.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But no, I never seen the lumberjack doing that, and I heard it. I'm sure it's true.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, you'd see one of them, like I mentioned about Ted Collins. Sure, you'd see Ted. He'd, you know, he'd run around sometimes because of the moonshine that somebody'd get from somebody. Have it ditched somewhere, you know, but not in the places, no. You wouldn't see them drinking it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much social life in Bowfill in the 20s when you first came?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. They had a moving picture show there, and they had shows,

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: oh, three nights a week, I suppose. And then the upstairs part was the dance floor. Dances two or three every month. Card parties, oh, yes, the social life there. And even during that depression, the social life was probably better than at any other time. They'd have dances there. As I say, nobody had to dime, you know. So the music would be volunteers. Schoolteacher, I know we had one in the school, played the violin. Someone else played something else. My wife played the piano. And it was all for free. And then for lunch, people bring little things, they put sandwiches on. You see, there's quite a lot of wild game around that area, deer and elk. There was a lot of meat coming in there. You make up sandwiches with that, you grind it up. Well, little things like that. And then they would pass the hat.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And everybody knew, now, if you don't have a nickel, for God's sake, don't use your last nickel to put it in. It was just to buy the coffee. That was the only thing that was the only expense, was to buy the coffee. Oh, they had good times. Good times. And then some, you see, was making home brew. And you would go to a house and have a bottle of beer.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah. I'd say the social life during that Depression. And then the people stayed together better. Well, before that, they had some of them, you know, with the cars. They'd go to Moscow to show us or something like that. That Depression come along. You didn't have that kind of money to drive cars. So they stayed at home and made their own entertainment. It was good. I've heard a lot of people talk about that afterwards. Really, socially, that was the best times of their life right there during that Depression. Well, you know, as they always say, misery likes company. Well, maybe that isn't a good definition. But just about everybody was broke, so I'm trying to get at it. So the guy next to you was no different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what does it say?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I don't know. You count the people on the payroll there on your two hands. I know because of the school teachers myself. A few people working, taking care of the horses. That was about it. Even the Superintendent Noggle, they put him down on such a low wage, he told me I wouldn't miss it if I never got it. But it was down and the company didn't know they'd ever get going again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Noggle doing then?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He was Superintendent. Same thing.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, he had not much to do at all then. Oh, they had buildings there. Big warehouse, office building, nobody in the office. It was a big warehouse where a lot of the stuff was stored. Well, he didn't have much to do, but this group of men that were taking care of the horses, he had to supervise them.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Buy a little hay and grain once in a while. I think they said there was 260 head of horses there. Well, they had quite a few camps around when they were working and every camp was about 120 men. I never did know how many horses were in each camp though. I knew they were not a man.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I was curious about the single lumberjacks that stuck around and were there logging for a number of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they sort of get adopted by families? Did they have real close ties where they became part of the social life of Beauville too?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, in some cases, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: In some cases, yes. The single lumberjack, he had special family, friends.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yes, that happened.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But when they talk about going to town, it was Spokane. That's the name of the town. Spokane. Always by train too in those days.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They had two ways of going there. By train, down on the W &M to Palouse and they had two chances there. Either the Great Northern and Northern Pacific gets Spokane or they could take the Milwaukee St. Mary's Inn. So they went both ways.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yeah, the people there were pretty good. I know one time there, this must have been about 1927, I'd say. We had a big fella, Charlie Albright. He died right on the basketball floor. He was having a town team basketball game there and I was the manager of the team. It was a nice night in the winter, February I think. We'd had a good team here and this was a playoff. The winner would be sort of declared the champion. I think we were playing Kendrick and the crowd, the gym was full. Nice night. This Charlie, he'd let us in time out and down he went. We got over there, a bunch of us, he wasn't coming too. Everybody had done everything they could, you know, artificial restoration. I remember we got a barrel in from outside the door. We put him on that road back. Everybody had an idea what to do. There was a couple of nurses in the room. Well, one nurse said, I can't feel any pulse. The other one, whether she could or not, she didn't say. But somebody, of course, we had a doctor's station in Bloemoland, Dr. Brown. So somebody tore out to get Dr. Brown. That place was getting panicky. The man was there and hadn't come to and nothing was being done. And then finally Dr. Brown showed up. Of course, it was just a foot. All the snow was off and he peeped in. He got inside the door. He said, well, settle down. He said, the doctor's here. The doctor's here now. And he walked over to him and examined him a little bit. Well, he said, we'll have to take him up to the, well, it used to be a hospital, but it wasn't operating then as a hospital, but that's where the doctor's office was. So he did say, we'll have to take him up to the hospital. It was a hospital building, see. So then they sent somebody for a stretcher, somewhere close, I guess. Got there with the stretcher and we put this charlie on and he was a big fella, about 240 pounds. And that snow, it was all there was was trails to walk in. And we had to change off a good many times because one would have to plow through the snow and while the other one walked the path and then they'd shift over just a little ways and they'd change off. And I remember I was wound up being one that was carrying him in the door. I'd been in interchange different times. I was one of them. And the doctor said, thanks boys. And he held up his hand and he said, his relatives will be here tomorrow. Well, he was a single man. He says, his relatives will be here tomorrow. Well, that's the first that any of us knew that Charlie was dead. I suppose we should have known more, but we didn't. We thought, you know, there was a chance that he was alive and would be. But that doctor knew all the time. But I think he handled it just right. Because the crowd, you know, was getting kind of panicky. Do something, do something. Well, everything was being done that could, but he died right there apparently. Because he never, never stirred. But then he had some or had lived at Palouse, I guess. So the funeral was scheduled for Palouse a couple of days later. And a special train, and a special train came up from Hotlatch and took the brick near the whole town down to Palouse to the funeral. And of course, that would bring them back. I'll never forget that day either. This engineer, Elmer Helm, he was a kind of guy. Well, he seen that big crowd on the depot platform. It was a steam engine, you know. And set up there, the train would run right on by the depot for half a mile or more. And then there was a Y out there that would Y the engine. And when he seen that crowd out there, it was this, well, he was kind of a showman. He must have given everything it had because he came by that depot and that old engine was just swaying, you know. He was putting as fast as he could, for his own reasons is all. But you know, it scared that crowd half to death. But still for all, they wanted to go to the funeral and they did. They got on there. But there were people that got sick on the way down. But I know they didn't get sick from the ride going down. They were so scared. Him counting. They didn't know what he'd be doing, you know, on the way down. But we all got down there and back. But now that's the way people would do there, you see. There's something like that. The whole town would turn out fine. There were good people. Real good people, generally speaking.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: First that ended the basketball for the season. And should have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the sports very big in Gold Hill?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They always had the baseball team. Always. And a pretty good one. And they'd have little leagues made up of... Well, Potlatch always had a strong team. Genesee always had a good outfit. They were in the league. Kendrick always had a good one. They were in Vauxhall. And sometimes Elk River would come up with a pretty good team. They had a sawmill up there in Elk River until about 1930, I think, that sawmill went out. When the sawmill was there, Elk River was a much larger town than Vauxhall was. And they had a pretty good baseball team. It went down after that, of course. Oh, yeah, Troy would have a team some years and some years they didn't. But we had a league. A real interest. And the townspeople would follow the team around the country. We'd go to St. Mary's sometimes and Mida and different places. But the league games are interchangeable with these other towns. Well, we have won the championship there and we've lost it to more times than we've won. But once in a while we'd come in with a winner. Yeah, that's a funny thing. Moscow couldn't support a town baseball team. Too much university there, I guess. For some reason Moscow never had a bit of a town baseball team.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: You'd think, you know, there'd be enough university students that would stay around there during the summer that they could have a team. But no. Sometimes we used to get some guys from the university to come up and we'd find them a job for the summer and they'd play ball with us. Red Jacoby was one of them. He was with us for years, I think. He later went on to Wyoming and he became a graduate manager there. In fact, he just died about a year ago.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I met a man here, spoke then, about a year ago. He was from Wyoming. I said, you know, I only know one man. I guess I probably said I only know one man in Wyoming. And that's Red Jacoby. He says, I know Red Jacoby. That's kind of funny, you know. As a whole state. And he did. You know, it was pretty good.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yeah, we had baseball, basketball, volleyball. But that isn't so today in all those towns now. They don't have a baseball team. Oh, there's probably some of them might have a basketball team, but it's never organized like it used to be. You know, we had, as I say, those leagues. And drawn up, we had rules and we had officials. Made up of the county.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That story about the doctor reminded me of that story that you told me about when your wife was having the baby.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me that?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, well, yes, that was when our youngest boy, Jack, was born on April 5, 1931. And we had a doctor in the local. I believe that was Dr. Porl. I wouldn't have swear to his name. I think it might have been Dr. Porl. He visited my wife on this day, which was Saturday, I think. Yes. And, oh, he said, don't worry about it. You've got a week or maybe two weeks yet. So, he told her that he was taking the train to Elk River that day and he'd be up there over the weekend. So, during that Saturday night here, the labor pains started and the baby was coming.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So, I hiked up to the doctor's office, which was the old hospital, and the doctor's wife come down with me and she did. I'm awful glad that she did come because she knew so much more about it than I did. But, of course, got a hold of the doctor by phone, Elk River. Well, he had no way of coming, of course. The road was impassable. Nobody could get over there. They couldn't call it a highway. It was just a road at the time, but they couldn't get over it. So, well, couldn't do anything about that. But, Axel Anderson, some way, he found out about that. I hadn't talked to Axel. He found out about it. And they had this old Dodge bug that they used to run on the railroad sometimes. Not but an old Dodge car with the four railroad wheels on it. But it had been put away for the winter and been used again that year, but the battery was out of it. He scouted around and got a battery and got in that old rig and got the doctor and brought him down to Volvo. I didn't even know they were coming. Here they showed up at the house. I was awfully glad to see them because, well, the doctor said everything was all right. Glad to hear that too, of course. But I was so excited. I didn't even ask Axel if he'd like a cup of coffee. I told him about it sometime afterwards. Oh, he says, I understand. I had kids too, you know. I always thanked Axel for that. He got the word. I don't know. I did find out. That's how he told them. This was late at night too.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah. You know, and then when the Depression came on hard to do worse, we didn't have a doctor. We didn't have a doctor in Volvo, but here they had 260 horses across there. And the Potlatch Lumber Company did have a veterinary. So you'd hear this around town. Horses are more important than the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people go to the vet when they got sick?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Quite often, especially if they got hurt, you know.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And he did have a reputation of, he could set a bone probably as good as a medical doctor. Oh, your wrist and legs and stuff. And he also knew a little something about medicine. Yeah. Of course, you know,

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: he couldn't practice that. He'd have them in jail or someplace. Oh yes, a lot of people take his advice. And I say for an injury, you go right ahead. Fix them up until, you know, they could get to a doctor.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Which you couldn't. That'd be the next day.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, that's the way that worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What his nickname was?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The Big Sweet. Yeah. Or anybody would say the Big Sweet. Nobody but Axel.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He was a pretty husky guy.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yes, yes he was. He was a real husky man.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, he was in the snow plow one time on one of their logging roads. And the darn snow plow derailed and went over a trussel is what it was. It went down there quite a ways. Had a stove in there and of course it, you know, fell over. One of the things didn't burn up. Axel got hurt pretty bad.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But, came out of it. I think he might have broke his shoulder. But, he was awful lucky that it wasn't worse. And another fellow with him, Joe Evans, he got banged up some too. He broke his leg or arm or something. He was laid up for a while.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, I bet you Axel has had a lot of experience in the woods.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I had a fellow there, his name was Joe Buck. Well, he was a, oh I don't know, a jack of all trades. But, he was kind of a painter too. But, he was working for the potlats at the time in what they called the basin. Which is between Boville and Elk River, but off. Please, some. They had, oh about three camps out there. I think A, B, C, D, maybe they had four camps out there. The logs were all transported into Elk River at the mill. But, then they had a big coal dump out there. And the coal would come into Elk River. Then they would have to be taken out to these camps with the Shea engines. Well, maybe four or five cars at a time. And this Joe Buck, he was working out there as an engine watchman. Well, that would be at night when the engines were tied up. Well, it seems like there was no crew around this Joe. He was kind of a strong union man too. They didn't know it though until then. And so it was this night and they were getting short on coal there and as the story goes, Axel asked him, Joe could you take the engine and run into Elk River and get the one car load of coal. Joe says, sure Axel, sure I can, but villi. And he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Axel didn't even can him?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh no.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And I think it was the same, I'm pretty sure it was Joe Buck. He was kind of a bull cook around the camp one time. And oh, there's little jobs, you know, maybe they have a little pump out here somewhere to pump water into the camp or other little duties. Well, we'll give you two hours for this or four hours for this and then cleaning out the bunk houses and whatnot. And then they were getting these extra duties on them, you know.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, how about an hour for that?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Anyway, it all added up to twenty -five hours. And Joe, he wondered how he was going to work that. Only twenty -four hours in the day. Well, I guess they got it straightened out. But that, you see, that would be kind of along the line of Jipo. Give you an hour's pay today to take care of this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if the Met consider Axel as a big management person or if they thought of him more as a worker like themselves?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No, I never know of Axel to do physical days of labor in his life. He was always some kind of a boss. I know him. No. And I don't know. He was in the United States Army World War I. But I don't know what he done just when he first came out. He may have worked in the woods then, but that would have been ahead of my time. But from the time I knew him, from 1925 on, he was always in the supervisory capacity. He was a foreman or an assistant foreman. Well, I don't know. But see, no, I never knew him as an assistant foreman. He was either a foreman or a higher ranking.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Foreman, and then you see what they called the walking boss. He was higher than the foreman. He was, and then ahead of him would be the superintendent.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But the walking boss was kind of a troubleshooter, you know, to get around all the camps. See what the foreman were doing. His work could mostly be with the foreman for the different camps. Or he'd hike out into the woods to see what was going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me what the responsibilities were of your job being depot agent there?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, it was just like a depot agent in any other place. I was telegraph operator, of course, and there I did the telegraphing as well.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: An agent is responsible for everything that happens in and around the station. And had to see that things were going right and have to keep in good touch, good relations with the business people. Of those days everything was handled on the railroad. There was no trucking. Trucking didn't come along quite a while afterwards. And then of course you're, well, the telegraph, you're manager of the Western Union and the express company. You're the express agent. Well, and with your help, you know, I have your cash here and there, you have to see that, supervise him and see that he's doing his work right. Same with your warehouse man. You do have to oversee those two. I did, anyway. And that would be the case. And, well, that's about it. Well, if you was at a station where there was a competitor, the railroad, then you was a railroad agent. A lot of your work would be to soliciting business. But that, at Bovill, it didn't have to do. Because it was, the two railroads were right there in the same building and going different directions. So there was no soliciting to do. But that is the, and of course the mail is handled on those trains. You're responsible for that mail while it's, until after it leaves the station. And you're responsible for just everything in and around the station.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And, well, after I stayed there so long, you see, and after a number of years, the managements of both railroads more or less left me alone. They didn't, very seldom they ever give me instructions of what had to be done that wasn't being done or anything like that. They kind of relied on me to run it up there. And I did the best I could. And I really enjoyed my work. I think one reason that I did this is because I got along quite well with the management.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I didn't try to bicker or fight with them. That's not pleasant work when you don't have bosses that you get along with. I didn't have any bosses on the job, but here like Spokane and Milwaukee, this was the headquarters for them, the Potlatch for the WI&M. But I got along with them good. And you did say you talked to Paul Gamble. He was my boss from the WI&M for a slight number of years. I got along well with him.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, that's... Probably you shouldn't, but then you'd do it anyway. One time there, a fellow died right across the meadow. And these people around us were from Spokane, and they wanted the body brought in Spokane. Again, this was in the winter, deep snow. But this undertaker, I'll call him Mortician, whatever you want to call him, came out from Spokane, and the draymen took him across the meadow to this place where the man had died. He'd been dead for a couple of years. A day and a half anyway, I guess. Well, his passenger train went to Elk River and back, which would take about two and a half hours. And the draymen took this man over, and he prepared the body over there well enough to ship what he thought it was. All he had was a basket, and they're narrow, you know. And then the draymen brought him back to the depot. And I should have known this, but I didn't. And here we had the body on the truck and ready to load into the baggage car. And this baggage man on that train, he all writes, Steve Kennard was his name, but he was one that wanted to stay right as strictly to the letter of the law and roll as he could. And when he seen the carrying basket, that's what they call it, and no handles on it, he said, no, no, no handles. Why, I said it can be handled easy enough. No handles on it. Well, I couldn't argue, you know, because there wasn't any handles on it. Well,

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I said, wouldn't see. So I went in the depot, and the conductor was still in there because Bowler was the register station and the conductors had to come in to register. And I told the conductor, he called up for a minute or so, yeah, he didn't know what I was doing. None of his business, no use in telling him. This I shouldn't have done, but I didn't have time to do it properly. But, you know, I'll have to answer that phone.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: You were just telling me about how you did a little short circuiting of the rules there on the dead man. You were talking to the conductor. Yeah, he said, okay, he waited a little bit.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, I sat down with the typewriter and wrote out a message addressed to this baggage man to accept this corpse as is and sign the superintendent's name to it. And I took it out and handed it to him. He said, that's good enough for me, the superintendent's name. He took the message, he took the corpse, and I never heard another word about it. And it was all right. Well, now this was on Saturday, see, and no trains on Sunday. If that body couldn't have been transported, it held that body someplace until Monday. And, you know, I would have done most anything. We got that corpse moving, and of course they could have, you know, put me on the carpet for that, had any trouble came from it. But no, it was all right. I didn't make a practice of doing those things. And I wouldn't do it for myself, but that one time I did that. Well, I didn't have the time. It takes time to get ahold of a superintendent, you know, and get this thing cleared. And I couldn't expect that conductor because passenger trains leave on a minute, you know. That's when they do leave, that's when they want to leave.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So that worked very good, I thought. No, I wasn't just exactly holding my breath for a few days afterwards, but I thought, baby, something might come. Nothing did.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No. So that's one of the things this particular agent done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference, were there real differences between the two lines besides the fact that one was organized by a lumber company and the way that the lines operated?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No. They all had the same rules, you know, all the trains run under train orders and timetables, and it's all just a good deal of the same system, and their accounting is just a good deal on the same order. Only there was one separate set of accounts for one railroad and one for the other. No. Oh, there's one brakeman sitting there one time arguing a little bit with a brakeman from the Milwaukee. He says, well, our railroad isn't as long as yours, but it's just as wide. Well, you see, in a way, a railroad's a railroad. And the WI&M was an interstate commerce road, too, because it was run into more than one state, see, from Washington and then into Idaho. And their plans, apparently, had been to go on to Montana. But fire or something in the area where they intended to go changed the plans. So they never got into Montana, but today, still, the name of the railroad is Washington, Idaho, and Montana.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, some of them used to say, will I make it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Or WI&M.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will I make it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then another fellow would say, maybe I will. Turn it around.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, there was another level of logs going out of that railroad in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which way did more logs go?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: To Potlatch.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Potlatch, see, had a good -sized mill there. One time, I don't know, with regard to the second largest in the country, I believe it was. I believe it was considered the second largest sawmill in the United States. But then, shortly after this, the bigger ones went in. The one at Lewiston was bigger. And I think Longview was the bigger one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did a lot of that same timber come back through when it was sawed up?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: A lot of it from the Potlatch Mill, come right back through Boville and go on east. Well, I think that was one of the reasons that Milwaukee bought that road. You see, before that, the business out of Potlatch was sort of divided up. Now, at Palouse, there's two railroads, the Northern Pacific and the Great Northern. And we would just say that each of them got a third, and then a third of it go to Boville for the Milwaukee. They didn't hold right onto those bases, but that, roughly speaking, you know, they divided it up. But then the Milwaukee buying the WI&M, well, now they get the lion's share of it, you know. I don't know how much they get, but probably 70%, I suppose, is the lumber that's cut at Potlatch and goes through Boville. And, generally speaking, on east. Most of it goes east.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Very, very few cartons of lumber out of Potlatch ever goes west. Well, these sawmills on the west coast, I guess, takes care of this area. But, east.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: So, Boville, being in that juncture point, sounds to me like it was really in a very central location for shipping logs. Oh, yes.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, the logs, you see, came in there and went to Potlatch. And we had a pole yard there, too, Cedar Pole. It's still going. They shipped a lot of poles out of there. Crease on them, you know. It used to be in the summertime we'd have quite a few sheep. Quite a few. Sometimes, you know, strictly sheep trained. They'd come in about Memorial Day. I think the agreements that they would have with the Forest Service to have the sheep on forest land, and that's where they mostly done their grazing, was June 1st. But they seemed to always get in about a day or so before. And they'd graze all summer until September, generally around Labor Day. They'd start shipping them out. And then train loads, you see, not car loads, but train loads. Mostly go to Chicago.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: You wouldn't know where they'd go to from there, but they had the yards there.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But that's all a thing of the past. No more sheep. Great in the country, it's all. I don't know why. Somebody told me why, but I didn't believe it. You know those sheep tenders, or sheep herders, or whatever you want to call them, a lot of them were Basques. More than they liked sheep, and they knew how to handle sheep. And just everybody can't. Oh yeah, an owner told me, oh it makes a big difference if you've got a man tending or herders that knows how, and one that doesn't. But this fellow told me why there's no more sheep anymore, because those fellows quit coming over here to herd them. And that's why they can't get them.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Whether it is or not, I don't know.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: With sheep, you've got to keep them on the move.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, isn't that funny?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, with hogs, it's always been my understanding, and I don't pretend to be any expert at all on that, but to keep hogs in a sort of a small pen, and feed them heavy. And that's where they put on the most weight. But with sheep, especially grazing, you've got to keep them going. The sheep will eat about so much and then lay down. Well, the herders, you can't let them do that. You've got to keep them moving. And when they move, they eat. That's funny. Just the opposite of the hogs. Keep them moving, and then they keep eating. Any time anyone still is at night.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: When you mentioned the seeder yard there, it reminded me of Leo. I was wondering if you could tell me what Leo was like. I've heard that he was a jokester. He certainly was a comic fellow.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, I'll tell you, he pulled a good one on a cousin of mine. This cousin, that was a long time ago. He was here in Spokane, and well, he had a little job of some kind. He was single, young. He didn't like the job he had, and I happened to be in town.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, I said, you want to come back with me to Bovill?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I said, I'm pretty sure you can go to work in the pole yard. All right, give it a try. So he did. And his job was to count the poles after a car was loaded. And then you count the poles, see how many is on there. They know how many is supposed to be on there. And of course the loaders and the checkers, whoever it is, tries to get exactly that number of poles on. But then they always have one to count them afterwards. Well, the best way to do it is go to the end where the butts are, you see. And you count those butts. You can use a little chalk mark if you want to, to know that you've counted that. And then you go to the other end. Well, oh yeah, sometimes you see the poles go that way. So the butts on each end, and the small parts would be to the middle. And this cousin, he was counting, that's part of his work that day. He was counting, and he'd get a different number on the other end. One more one. And he'd done it, he'd done it so many times that it was driving him crazy. Like each time you count on one end and maybe get a hundred poles, and the other end was 101. On that dog gun, Leo, there was a little open space there where there was not. And around the City Yard door there's lots of little cut off pieces, you know. He took one of those little short pieces, two feet long, and he shoved it in that space. That made the... Yeah. He'd do things like that.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He was, for quite a few years, he was in charge of the crease on him. That was part of his work there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that story you told me about getting his eggs stoned?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, yeah. He had a barn, he had a cow and some chickens, and he knew that he was losing some eggs. Well, he had a neighbor. He was a good enough fellow, but he was different. Buck Chambers. And he figured that probably Buck was stealing his eggs, but he hadn't seen them do it. So this time, it was early in the morning, I guess it was. I think he'd seen Buck go into the barn. And that was nothing to say, you know, or reprimand him or anything, or being in there. That was all right. But then Leo went in and, oh, yeah, Buck, Buck stuttered, you know. And Leo then he sized him up and he walked over to him. And I don't know. On the pockets, you could hear the eggs breaking, and he'd come down on top of his head just playful, you know, down on top of the head. Well, the jig was up then, of course. He didn't do anything about it. He didn't want to do anything about it. He just wanted to stop Buck from stealing his eggs.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, he was, well, stories used to come up around Bovil there.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And then they'd say, where did all these new stories come from? And somebody'd say, well, don't you know?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Leo Gilfoy makes those up.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I don't know whether he did or not. But, oh, yeah, he was, Leo, to me, didn't seem to ever worry about anything. Maybe that's why he lived so long.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He kept it to himself. It wasn't just, couldn't have been just the easy life, you know, after his wife couldn't be home to take care of the family. You had those three kids, you had to earn the livelihood as well as supervise the kids until they could do for themselves. It was a big job. A bigger job than most men do, I think.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He did a really good job. Really good.
